items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.topic: how chunking improves working memory capacity
items.transcript: Working memory is the mental workspace where you hold and manipulate information for a few seconds, a phone number you're dialing, a sentence you are reading, the steps of a math problem. And it turns out this workspace is remarkably limited. In the nineteen-fifties, psychologist George Miller estimated that most people can juggle only about seven separate items at once, give or take two. So how do expert waiters memorize a dozen dinner orders, or chess masters recall entire boards at a glance? The answer is a process called chunking. Chunking means grouping individual pieces of information into larger, meaningful units. Consider the letters F, B, I, C, I, A, N, A, S, A. As ten separate letters, that string overwhelms your working memory. But regroup them into FBI, CIA, NASA, and suddenly you are holding just three chunks instead of ten. Each chunk still contains the same letters, yet it occupies only one slot. Researchers demonstrated this with a college student who trained for months to repeat back long strings of random digits. An average person manages about seven digits; this student eventually reached over eighty. His secret wasn't a bigger memory. He was a competitive runner, so he recoded the digits into race times, three or four numbers at a time. The key insight is that chunking does not expand the number of slots in working memory. It expands what each slot can hold. This is why expertise matters so much: the more you know about a subject, the larger and more efficient your chunks become. A novice sees scattered details, while an expert sees a few rich, organized patterns.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How grouping information lets people overcome working memory limits
options.B: The story of a student who memorized over eighty digits
options.C: Why long-term memories fade more slowly than short-term ones
options.D: How government agencies such as the FBI train new staff

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是chunking（组块）如何让有限的工作记忆容纳更多信息，A准确概括。B只是讲座中的一个例子，属于too_narrow，抓住细节而非主旨。C谈长期记忆与短期记忆的消退速度，讲座根本没讨论，属于wrong_topic。D利用了讲座里出现的FBI等词，但方向完全错误，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the letters FBI, CIA, and NASA?

options.A: To show that some acronyms are easier to pronounce
options.B: To illustrate how regrouping letters reduces the memory load
options.C: To point out that the string contains exactly ten letters
options.D: To argue that famous organizations are easier to remember

questions.explanation: 教授用FBI、CIA、NASA说明把十个孤立字母重组成三个有意义的组块能大幅减轻记忆负担，B正确点明其目的。A谈发音难易，误判目的，属于wrong_purpose。C只是复述讲座提到的十个字母这一事实，没解释为什么，属于literal_restatement。D把例子引申为知名机构更好记，是教授未表达的推断，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that the digit-memorizing student was a competitive runner?

options.A: To suggest that regular exercise improves overall memory
options.B: To prove that anyone can memorize over eighty random digits
options.C: To explain how he chunked the digits into familiar race times
options.D: To describe how many digits the student could finally repeat

questions.explanation: 教授提到学生是赛跑运动员，是为了说明他把数字重新编码成熟悉的比赛成绩来实现组块，C正确。A把跑步理解成锻炼提升记忆，误判目的，属于wrong_purpose。B说任何人都能记八十位数字，夸大成普遍结论，属于over_inference。D只是复述学生能背多少位数字这一内容，没解释运动员身份的作用，属于literal_restatement。

questions.stem: Why does the professor contrast a novice and an expert at the end of the lecture?

options.A: To repeat that experts simply know more about their subject
options.B: To recommend that students focus on a single field of study
options.C: To claim that a novice cannot form any useful chunks at all
options.D: To show that expertise produces larger, more efficient chunks

questions.explanation: 结尾对比新手与专家，是要说明知识越丰富，组块越大越高效，D正确。A只是复述专家懂得更多这一表面内容，没触及组块这个要点，属于literal_restatement。B建议学生专注单一领域，是教授没有提出的目的，属于wrong_purpose。C说新手完全无法形成组块，过度绝对化，属于over_inference。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how folk melodies were absorbed into nationalist compositions
items.transcript: In the nineteenth century, a powerful idea swept through European music: that a composer's work should capture the spirit of a particular nation. This movement is known as musical nationalism, and one of its central techniques was drawing on folk melodies, the traditional songs of ordinary people in the countryside. Composers wanted their symphonies and operas to sound distinctly Norwegian, or Czech, or Hungarian, rather than simply echoing the international style centered in Vienna and Paris. Now, absorbing folk material was rarely as simple as copying a tune note for note. Take the Hungarian composer Béla Bartók. Early in the twentieth century, he traveled through remote villages with a phonograph, recording thousands of peasant songs before they vanished. But when he used them in his compositions, he transformed them, reharmonizing the melodies with modern, dissonant chords the original singers would never have imagined. The folk tune supplied the raw material; the composer supplied a new frame. Interestingly, some so-called folk themes weren't authentic at all. Certain composers simply invented melodies in a folk style, and audiences couldn't tell the difference, accepting them as genuine national heritage. This reveals something important: what mattered was not strict authenticity but the feeling of national identity the music evoked. By weaving these melodies, real or imagined, into concert works, composers helped forge a sense of shared cultural belonging at a time when many nations were struggling to define themselves. The folk song, once sung in a village square, became a symbol of the nation on the grand stage of the concert hall.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Béla Bartók recorded peasant songs using a phonograph
options.B: How composers used folk melodies to express national identity
options.C: Why the international style dominated concert halls in Vienna
options.D: How villagers traditionally performed songs in the countryside

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是十九世纪作曲家如何借用民歌来表达民族身份，即音乐民族主义，B正确。A中的Bartók录音只是一个具体例子，属于too_narrow。C谈维也纳国际风格的主导地位，恰恰是讲座中作曲家想要摆脱的对象，属于wrong_topic。D谈村民如何演唱歌曲，讲座并未展开，只是借用了民歌相关词汇，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Bartók do with the folk songs he collected?

options.A: He published them exactly as the villagers had sung them
options.B: He taught the original melodies to students in Vienna
options.C: He reharmonized them with modern, dissonant chords
options.D: He simplified them to match traditional peasant singing

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说Bartók用现代的、不协和的和弦重新和声化这些民歌，C正确。A说他原封不动地发表，与讲座中他对旋律进行改造相矛盾，属于wrong_detail。B说他在维也纳教这些旋律，讲座没提，属于topic_adjacent。D说他把民歌简化以贴合传统演唱，与他添加复杂现代和声正好相反，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that some folk themes were actually invented?

options.A: To criticize composers for deceiving their concert audiences
options.B: To note that certain melodies were newly composed
options.C: To argue that authentic folk songs had no real value
options.D: To show that the feeling of identity mattered more than authenticity

questions.explanation: 教授提到有些民歌其实是编造的，是为了说明真正重要的不是真伪，而是音乐唤起的民族认同感，D正确。A把它读成谴责作曲家欺骗听众，误判目的，属于wrong_purpose。B只是复述某些旋律是新创作的这一事实，没解释用意，属于literal_restatement。C说真正的民歌毫无价值，是教授没有表达的过度引申，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about musical nationalism?

options.A: It valued perceived national character over strict historical accuracy
options.B: It required every composer to abandon the international style
options.C: It preferred unaltered folk tunes to reworked concert pieces
options.D: It emerged mainly to preserve dying village traditions

questions.explanation: 由讲座可推断音乐民族主义看重的是所感受到的民族气质，而非严格的历史真实性，A正确。B说要求每位作曲家彻底放弃国际风格，过度绝对化，属于over_generalization。C说它更偏爱未经改动的民歌而非改编的音乐会作品，与Bartók等人的做法相反，属于reversed_logic。D说它主要是为了保护濒临消失的乡村传统，是相关但讲座未支持的推断，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: the anthropology of food taboos across societies
items.transcript: Every human society draws a line between what counts as food and what does not. A substance that one culture treats as a delicacy, another may find utterly repulsive. These prohibitions are what anthropologists call food taboos, and they are far more revealing than they first appear. A food taboo is a cultural rule forbidding the eating of certain animals or plants, even when those items are nutritious and readily available. Consider the pig. In several Middle Eastern traditions, pork is strictly forbidden, while in parts of East Asia it's a dietary staple. Why the difference? Anthropologists have offered two competing explanations. The materialist view, associated with Marvin Harris, argues that taboos make practical sense: pigs compete with humans for grain and water and are poorly suited to hot, arid climates, so avoiding them was economically sound. The symbolic view, associated with Mary Douglas, takes a different angle. She argued that cultures sort animals into orderly categories, and creatures that don't fit neatly, that seem to cross boundaries, get labeled unclean. A pig, which has cloven hooves but does not chew its cud, is in this framework an anomaly. What both approaches share is the recognition that taboos are not arbitrary quirks. They encode a group's environment, its history, and its way of organizing the world. When you learn why a community refuses a particular food, you are really learning how it draws the boundaries of its own identity. That is why an anthropologist studying a single dietary rule can end up uncovering an entire worldview.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why pigs are specifically avoided in some Middle Eastern traditions
options.B: How different climates determine which crops a society grows
options.C: What food taboos reveal about a culture's worldview
options.D: How anthropologists sort animals into physical classification categories

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是食物禁忌能揭示一种文化的价值观和世界观，C正确。A中的猪肉禁忌只是用来说明的例子，属于too_narrow。B谈气候如何决定种植的作物，讲座只是顺带提到气候，主题并非农业，属于wrong_topic。D借用讲座中分类动物的说法，但那只是Douglas理论的一部分，不是主旨，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: Why does the professor point out that pork is forbidden in some regions but a staple in others?

options.A: To show that pigs are healthier when raised in certain climates
options.B: To argue that Middle Eastern diets are generally less varied
options.C: To state that different regions eat different amounts of pork
options.D: To highlight that the same food is treated differently across cultures

questions.explanation: 教授对比猪肉在不同地区的地位，是为了突出同一种食物在不同文化中待遇迥异，从而引出禁忌值得研究，D正确。A谈猪在某些气候更健康，误判目的，属于wrong_purpose。B说中东饮食更单调，是讲座没有表达的引申，属于over_inference。C只是复述不同地区吃的猪肉多少不同这一表面内容，属于literal_restatement。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the pig as an 'anomaly' in Mary Douglas's framework?

options.A: To illustrate how creatures that cross categories get labeled unclean
options.B: To prove that pigs are biologically unusual among farm animals
options.C: To note that a pig has cloven hooves but no cud
options.D: To suggest that all boundary-crossing animals are dangerous

questions.explanation: 把猪称为异常，是为了说明Douglas理论中跨越分类边界的动物会被视为不洁，A正确。B说猪在生物学上不寻常，误把象征分类当成生物特征，属于wrong_purpose。C只是复述猪有偶蹄却不反刍这一事实，没解释用意，属于literal_restatement。D说所有跨界动物都危险，过度引申出教授未表达的结论，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about studying a single food taboo?

options.A: It always reveals the complete history of an entire nation
options.B: It can reveal how a culture organizes its world
options.C: It shows that taboos are mostly random personal preferences
options.D: It matters only to specialists working in nutrition science

questions.explanation: 由讲座可推断，研究一条饮食规则能揭示一个文化组织世界、划定身份边界的方式，B正确。A说总能揭示整个民族的完整历史，过度夸大，属于over_generalization。C说禁忌大多是随机的个人偏好，与讲座强调禁忌并非任意相反，属于reversed_logic。D说这只对营养学专家有意义，是相关但讲座未支持的推断，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: linguistics
items.topic: how language shapes the way we perceive color
items.transcript: Look at a rainbow, and you might assume everyone sees exactly the same bands of color. But the words a language gives us for color can actually influence how quickly we notice differences between shades. This idea connects to a broader theory in linguistics called linguistic relativity, the notion that the language we speak can shape aspects of how we perceive the world. Color is one of the clearest testing grounds. English treats blue as a single category, but Russian has no single word for it. Instead, Russian speakers must distinguish goluboy, a light blue, from siniy, a dark blue, the way English speakers separate pink from red. Researchers wondered whether this linguistic difference affects perception itself. In one well-known study, participants looked at squares of blue and had to quickly pick which of two shades matched a target. Russian speakers were measurably faster than English speakers at telling light blue from dark blue, because their language forces them to mark that boundary. When the two shades fell within a single Russian category, that advantage disappeared. Now, this doesn't mean English speakers can't see the difference. Of course they can. The effect is subtle, a matter of milliseconds. But it suggests that the categories built into our vocabulary act like a lens, nudging our attention toward certain distinctions. Language doesn't trap us in a fixed way of seeing, but it does gently tune what we notice first. And color is only the beginning. Researchers apply the very same questions to how different languages carve up space, time, and even emotion.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Russian separates light blue from dark blue
options.B: Why rainbows contain a fixed number of color bands
options.C: How people match colored squares in laboratory experiments
options.D: How the words a language offers can influence color perception

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是语言提供的颜色词汇如何影响我们对颜色的感知，即语言相对论，D正确。A中俄语区分深浅蓝只是支撑主旨的例子，属于too_narrow。B谈彩虹有固定数量的色带，讲座恰恰质疑人人所见相同，属于wrong_topic。C借用讲座中匹配色块实验的说法，但那只是研究方法，不是主旨，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how do Russian speakers differ from English speakers regarding blue?

options.A: They use two separate words for shades of blue
options.B: They can see far more shades of blue than English speakers
options.C: They learn their color words later than English-speaking children
options.D: They group all blues together under one single word

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说俄语用goluboy和siniy两个不同的词分别指浅蓝和深蓝，A正确。B说俄语者能看到更多蓝色色调，讲座只说他们辨别更快而非能看到更多，属于wrong_detail。C谈颜色词的习得年龄，讲座没提，属于topic_adjacent。D说俄语把所有蓝色归为一个词，与讲座所述正好相反，那其实是英语的做法，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare goluboy and siniy to pink and red?

options.A: To show that Russian has many more color words than English
options.B: To help English speakers grasp how Russian splits up blue
options.C: To argue that pink and red are basic universal colors
options.D: To note that Russian has two different words for blue

questions.explanation: 教授拿粉色和红色作比，是为了帮英语者理解俄语像区分粉红与红那样把蓝色一分为二，B正确。A说俄语颜色词整体远多于英语，讲座没这么说，误判目的，属于wrong_purpose。C说粉色和红色是基本的普遍颜色，是过度引申，属于over_inference。D只是复述俄语有两个表示蓝色的词这一事实，没解释类比的用意，属于literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the influence of language on perception?

options.A: It completely determines which colors people are able to see
options.B: It affects vocabulary learning more than it affects eyesight
options.C: It gently guides attention rather than fixing how we see
options.D: It has no measurable effect on how people perceive color

questions.explanation: 由讲座可推断，语言只是像镜头一样轻微引导注意力，而非固定我们的感知，C正确。A说语言完全决定人能看到哪些颜色，过度夸大，属于over_generalization。B说它影响词汇学习多于影响视觉，是相关但讲座未支持的推断，属于topic_adjacent。D说语言对感知毫无可测量的影响，与实验中俄语者反应更快的结果相反，属于reversed_logic。
